evidence_quotes.quote: claiming that they hold the right to withdraw the sold product from the user

paragraphs.text: Hi! Thank you for reading my feedback! I think there is an additional issue that could be addressed in this act relating to consumer protection and preservation of digital media. There is currently a practice of companies selling software and games to consumers while claiming that they hold the right to withdraw the sold product from the user when they don't see the need to support it any longer. This means that the product was not actually sold in itself but as a license to use. On one end, the companies use deceptive language (similar to dark design patterns) to make it seem like the consumer has ownership of the product. In practice, as was said, they actually sell licenses for use which is not what they market it as. If the products are to be sold and not licensed, companies should be forced to disclose how the product will be usable after they stop supporting it and provide the means to access the product in some way and to some extent as well. This could for example be to move the product to an open gaming license or such or allow offline use of the product. On the other end, this prevents the user for using what they have bought to the extent that they might wish, which in the long run prevents preservation of digital media. Archival and preservation of digital media has already become an issue in the few decades that computers have been in normal consumers homes. Old media formats and such are quickly becoming outdated and the expiration date of digital media storage is limited as it is. Allowing companies to fuel further disruptive practices will further hamper the preservation of digital media. This will lead to a gap in our history of digital media for the future and through that negativly impact future research on digital media and future software development. Contemporary scholars, such as Bret Victor and Alan Kay, has alluded to how this has already begun happening where coding and software research from the 70s and 80s has been forgotten due to companies not acting to support its preservation or acting actively against there proliferation. Protecting consumers rights to buying digital media, software, and games is implying indefinite ownership of the bought product. Comparing this to other products, the book publisher or author will not come and destroy books that I buy. The clothing brands and stored wont destroy clothes I buy once they are out of fashion. But software and cloud companies and game publishers and storefronts take that possiblity for granted and are currently abusing it to profit from consumers. Thank you again for reading my thoughts on the matter. This is just two of many reasons that this needs to be addressed. Regardless if this is included or not, as someone who works with human computer interaction, this act is of great interest to me and I value what is proposed in it regardless!
